description: SkillsAllYouNeed — open registry of AI skills for Claude, Claude Code, ChatGPT, Gemini, Perplexity, Copilot, OpenCode, and Codex.

skills.name: Artifacts
skills.description: Standalone code or document outputs that render live in a dedicated side panel — HTML, React, SVG, Markdown, or Mermaid.
skills.trigger: “Build me a React calculator” · “Make me an SVG diagram”
skills.example: A request for an interactive component opens a side panel with a runnable preview the user can iterate on.

skills.name: Visualizer
skills.description: Inline SVG diagrams, charts, and interactive widgets streamed directly into the body of a chat reply.
skills.trigger: “Show me a chart of…” · “Draw a flow diagram of…”
skills.example: A complexity question is answered with an inline annotated SVG instead of plain prose.

skills.name: Code Execution
skills.description: Sandboxed Linux container that runs code and produces real files (.docx, .pptx, .xlsx, .pdf, images).
skills.trigger: “Run this Python” · “Generate a PPTX from this outline”
skills.example: Claude executes a pandas notebook against an uploaded CSV and returns a real .xlsx report.

skills.name: Skills
skills.description: Modular instruction packs Claude loads on demand — docx, pptx, xlsx, pdf, frontend‑design, brainstorming, skill‑creator and more.
skills.example: “Make me a deck about Q2” loads the pptx skill, which knows how to produce a real, editable presentation.

skills.name: Projects
skills.description: Persistent workspaces with custom instructions and a shared knowledge base of uploaded files.
skills.trigger: Create from the sidebar; pin chats inside
skills.example: A “Q2 Planning” project keeps the brief, the deck, and prior chats together with consistent custom instructions.

skills.name: Memory
skills.description: Persistent, user‑editable facts that travel across every conversation.
skills.example: Claude recalls your stack, tone preferences, and timezone in every new chat without re‑priming.

skills.name: Past Chat Search
skills.description: Built‑in conversation_search and recent_chats tools let Claude reference prior conversations.
skills.trigger: “What did we decide about… last week?”
skills.example: Claude finds a prior thread and quotes the conclusion verbatim in a new chat.

skills.name: Web Search
skills.description: Search the live web for current information, with attributed sources.
skills.trigger: Any question with a freshness need
skills.example: “What's the latest in the case of X v. Y?” returns a synthesised answer with cited sources.

skills.name: MCP Connectors
skills.description: Third‑party app integrations — Google Drive, Gmail, Slack, GitHub, Asana, Notion, Linear, HubSpot, Zapier and more.
skills.example: “Summarise this morning's Linear updates and email Maria a digest” chains MCP servers end‑to‑end.

skills.name: Styles
skills.description: Custom output personalities — concise, formal, code‑first — applied per chat or globally.
skills.trigger: Sidebar → choose or author a Style
skills.example: A “Brutally Concise” style strips every preamble from every reply.

skills.name: Image generation
skills.description: Image creation through connected services (e.g. via MCP), surfaced inline in Claude.
skills.example: A request for a hero illustration returns a rendered PNG inside the conversation.

skills.name: Voice mode
skills.description: Spoken back‑and‑forth conversation on the mobile app.
skills.example: A walking conversation about a draft, with Claude responding in natural voice.

skills.name: Terminal CLI
skills.description: Agentic coding from the command line — read, write, run, commit, all inside your terminal.
skills.example: Ask for a refactor and Claude Code edits files in place, runs tests, and shows a diff before committing.

skills.name: Plan Mode
skills.description: Read‑only exploration phase that produces a plan before any change is made to your repo.
skills.example: On a large refactor request, Claude maps the call graph first, then proposes a step‑by‑step diff plan.

skills.name: Subagents
skills.description: Spawn specialised child agents (verifier, researcher, formatter) with their own scoped context.
skills.trigger: From within a plan or hook
skills.example: A verifier subagent reviews the main agent's output in a fresh context window and reports back.

skills.name: Hooks
skills.description: Lifecycle scripts — PreToolUse, PostToolUse, SessionStart — that fire deterministically around agent steps.
skills.example: A PostToolUse hook runs eslint --fix every time the agent writes a JS file.

skills.name: Slash Commands
skills.description: Reusable, parameterised prompt templates invoked with /command.
skills.example: /review @file generates a structured code review every time without re‑writing the prompt.

skills.name: CLAUDE.md
skills.description: Repo‑level instruction file Claude Code reads on every session to learn your conventions.
skills.trigger: Drop a CLAUDE.md at repo root
skills.example: A CLAUDE.md pins your test runner, module aliases, and the “no any types” rule across all sessions.

skills.name: MCP Servers
skills.description: Connect to external tools via Model Context Protocol — databases, ticket trackers, search APIs.
skills.example: A Postgres MCP lets Claude Code run SELECT queries against your dev database during a debug session.

skills.name: VS Code Extension
skills.description: Claude Code rendered as a side panel inside Visual Studio Code with diff‑aware UI.
skills.example: A diff appears inline in the editor; you accept or reject hunks visually.

skills.name: JetBrains Plugin
skills.description: Claude Code inside IntelliJ, PyCharm, GoLand, WebStorm, and the rest of the JetBrains family.
skills.example: Trigger Claude on a failing test from the gutter; it proposes a patch in‑line.

skills.name: Claude Code for Slack
skills.description: Kick off coding tasks from any Slack channel; results post back as threaded replies.
skills.example: A bug report in #frontend is handed to @claude, which opens a PR and links it back in‑thread.

skills.name: GitHub Actions
skills.description: Run Claude Code inside CI/CD pipelines — review PRs, triage issues, generate release notes.
skills.example: Every PR gets an automated review comment with concrete change suggestions.

skills.name: Canvas
skills.description: Side‑panel collaborative document and code editor with inline review comments.
skills.example: A draft essay is opened in canvas where ChatGPT can suggest targeted edits to specific paragraphs.

skills.name: Custom GPTs
skills.description: User‑authored assistants with custom instructions, knowledge files, and tool selection.
skills.example: A “Tax FAQ” GPT with the firm's policy PDFs answers staff questions in a consistent voice.

skills.name: Actions
skills.description: REST API calls from a Custom GPT, declared via an OpenAPI schema and authenticated per‑call.
skills.example: A travel GPT calls a flight‑search API live during the chat to return real itineraries.

skills.name: DALL·E 3
skills.description: Native image generation built into the chat surface, with iterative editing.
skills.example: A brief is turned into a hero image, then nudged with “same composition, warmer light”.

skills.name: Advanced Data Analysis
skills.description: Python sandbox for working with uploaded files — pandas, matplotlib, scikit‑learn, ffmpeg.
skills.example: A CSV upload is profiled, cleaned, and charted, with each step shown as runnable code.

skills.name: Browsing
skills.description: Built‑in web search and page fetch, citing sources in‑line.
skills.example: A research question returns a synthesised answer with footnote‑style citations.

skills.name: Memory
skills.description: Persistent facts the model remembers across chats; user‑editable from settings.
skills.example: The model recalls your industry and writing voice across new conversations.

skills.name: Projects
skills.description: Folders that group chats with their own custom instructions and shared files.
skills.example: A “Thesis” project keeps every draft, reference PDF, and reviewer chat in one place.

skills.name: Connectors
skills.description: First‑party integrations with Google Drive, GitHub, Outlook, SharePoint, Box, Dropbox, Gmail, Linear and more.
skills.example: “Find the Q3 deck in my Drive and summarise the financial section” pulls live from Drive.

skills.name: Voice Mode (Advanced)
skills.description: Real‑time, low‑latency voice conversation with intonation and interruption.
skills.example: A hands‑free brainstorm in the car; ChatGPT pauses when interrupted and picks up where it left off.

skills.name: GPT Store
skills.description: Public marketplace of Custom GPTs published by other users and partners.
skills.example: A user installs a public “Code Tutor” GPT instead of building their own.

skills.name: Sora
skills.description: Video generation, where regionally available, surfaced from inside the chat surface.
skills.example: A scene description returns a short generated video clip rendered into the chat.

skills.name: Operator / Agent Mode
skills.description: Browser‑driving agent that completes multi‑step tasks on the live web on the user's behalf.
skills.example: “Book me a window seat on the cheapest direct flight” is carried out across multiple airline sites.

skills.name: Tasks / Scheduled
skills.description: Recurring or scheduled prompts that fire on a cadence and post their result back to the chat.
skills.example: A weekly Monday brief summarising the user's calendar and inbox arrives on its own.

skills.name: Research
skills.description: Multi‑step agentic research across the live web, returning a structured report with citations.
skills.trigger: Research mode toggle in the composer
skills.example: “Research the state of small‑molecule GLP‑1 agonists.” returns a multi‑section brief with linked sources.

skills.name: Computer Use
skills.description: Claude controls a real browser or desktop on the user's behalf via screenshots and mouse/keyboard input.
skills.trigger: Claude in Chrome · Computer Use API
skills.example: “File this expense in Concur” is carried out by Claude visibly driving the browser.

skills.name: Files in chat
skills.description: Drag‑and‑drop PDFs, DOCX, XLSX, images, audio, and code; the full content is parsed into context.
skills.trigger: Drag onto the composer · paperclip icon
skills.example: A 200‑page PDF is dropped in, and Claude answers questions citing specific page numbers.

skills.name: Imagine
skills.description: Generates interactive UI — sliders, forms, calculators — on the fly from a single prompt, inline.
skills.example: “Imagine a mortgage calculator with PMI” renders a working widget inside the reply.

skills.name: Inline widgets
skills.description: First‑party UI cards — recipe, map, weather, message composer, places, image search, sports — rendered inline.
skills.trigger: Triggered by intent (e.g. recipe or weather query)
skills.example: “Weekend in Lisbon” surfaces a places card with photos and a map alongside the prose answer.

skills.name: Claude for Excel
skills.description: Spreadsheet agent installed inside Microsoft Excel — edits formulas, builds models, explains cells.
skills.example: “Build a 5‑year DCF from this revenue tab” is executed in the workbook with auditable formulas.

skills.name: Claude in Chrome
skills.description: Browser extension that lets Claude read the active tab, drive forms, and complete multi‑tab workflows.
skills.example: “Reorder the same coffee as last Tuesday” completes the basket and checkout without further prompting.

skills.name: Cowork
skills.description: Desktop file and task automation aimed at non‑developers — batch rename, reorganize, OCR, summarise.
skills.example: “Move every invoice older than 90 days into /Archive” runs as a vetted file action on the user's Mac.

skills.name: Incognito Conversations
skills.description: Chats that bypass Memory and aren't retained after the window closes — useful for sensitive drafts.
skills.example: A salary negotiation draft is iterated on without polluting the user's persistent memory.

skills.name: Output Styles
skills.description: Configurable agent voice and format presets that pin Claude Code's tone and verbosity per project.
skills.example: A “diff‑only, no preamble” style strips every explanation in a busy repo.

skills.name: Permission Modes
skills.description: Plan / Accept‑Edits / Bypass / Auto — fine‑grained control over what the agent may do without prompting.
skills.example: Accept‑Edits lets Claude make file changes freely, but still requires approval to run shell commands.

skills.name: Background Tasks
skills.description: Long‑running tasks spawned with & that continue while you keep working; they report back on completion.
skills.example: “Run the full test suite & ” returns control immediately and posts the result when finished.

skills.name: Claude Code Skills
skills.description: Reusable agent skills identical in shape to Claude.ai Skills, scoped to a repo or user profile.
skills.example: A `release-notes` skill always assembles the changelog the way the team likes it.

skills.name: Usage & Cost tracking
skills.description: /cost shows tokens and dollars spent per session; /compact reduces context; /clear resets the window.
skills.example: At the end of a long session, /cost reports a $1.42 spend and which tools consumed the most tokens.

skills.name: Pulse
skills.description: Proactive daily brief that draws on Memory, Projects, and Connectors to deliver an unprompted morning digest.
skills.example: Each morning a card lists overnight calendar changes, key emails, and follow‑ups Pulse thinks you'll want.

skills.name: Study Mode
skills.description: Socratic‑tutor mode that withholds answers and instead guides the user through derivations and checks.
skills.example: A linear‑algebra question is met with leading sub‑questions instead of a direct solution.

skills.name: Record Mode
skills.description: Live meeting recording with real‑time transcription and a structured notes summary at the end.
skills.example: A 30‑minute call yields a transcript, action items, and a one‑paragraph executive summary.

skills.name: Shopping Research
skills.description: Comparison shopping with structured product cards — specs, prices, reviews surfaced inline.
skills.trigger: “Compare A vs B vs C” · product intent
skills.example: “Best sub‑$2,000 OLED TV for a bright room” returns a compact comparison card with pros and cons.

skills.name: Library
skills.description: Personal gallery of generated images and creations, organised by chat, with re‑edit and download.
skills.example: Every DALL·E 3 image from the past month is browseable in a grid and re‑openable in canvas.

skills.name: Realtime API
skills.description: Developer‑facing voice‑to‑voice low‑latency API powering custom real‑time agents.
skills.example: A customer‑support voice agent responds with sub‑second latency and natural barge‑in.

skills.name: Assistants API
skills.description: Predecessor to the Responses API — a server‑side primitive for stateful tool‑using agents.
skills.example: Existing Assistants deployments remain accessible but new builds should target Responses.

skills.name: Responses API
skills.description: Successor to Chat Completions designed for agentic flows — stateful, tool‑aware, multi‑turn.
skills.example: A multi‑tool agent loop is expressed as a single Responses call instead of a hand‑rolled state machine.

skills.name: Structured Outputs
skills.description: Strict JSON‑Schema‑enforced outputs that never violate the supplied schema.
skills.example: A pricing extractor returns a guaranteed‑valid object that parses cleanly into a typed struct.

skills.name: Anthropic Computer Use API
skills.description: API surface that exposes Computer Use — screenshots in, mouse/keyboard out — to any application.
skills.example: A vendor builds a QA bot that drives a staging site end‑to‑end through the Computer Use API.

skills.name: Anthropic Files API
skills.description: Upload, reuse, and reference files across Messages and Batch requests without re‑transmitting bytes.
skills.example: A 40 MB transcript is uploaded once and referenced by ID in dozens of downstream summarisation calls.

skills.name: Anthropic Message Batches API
skills.description: Submit thousands of Messages requests as a batch at substantially reduced cost and 24‑hour SLA.
skills.example: An overnight classification job of 100k records is submitted as a single batch at 50% price.

skills.name: Anthropic Prompt Caching
skills.description: Cache long system prompts and documents across calls to slash latency and per‑token cost.
skills.example: A 50k‑token system prompt is cached once and reused across a thousand follow‑up turns.

skills.name: Anthropic PDF support
skills.description: Native PDF understanding in the Messages API — text and visual layout, no separate OCR step.
skills.example: A multi‑column scientific paper is summarised with figure references preserved.

skills.name: Anthropic MCP Connector API
skills.description: Server‑side connector hosting that lets Claude reach MCP servers without the client managing transport.
skills.example: An app gives Claude a hosted Jira MCP without shipping any custom transport code.

skills.name: OpenAI Realtime API
skills.description: Bi‑directional voice‑to‑voice API with sub‑second latency and interruption support.
skills.example: A drive‑thru voice ordering kiosk responds in real time, with natural barge‑in.

skills.name: OpenAI Batch API
skills.description: Asynchronous batch submission of completions and embeddings at half the per‑token price.
skills.example: A million‑row labelling pass is submitted as a JSONL file and returned within the SLA window.

skills.name: OpenAI Vision API
skills.description: Multimodal image understanding through the Responses and Chat APIs — inline image_url inputs.
skills.example: A receipt photo is OCR'd, line‑itemised, and posted into an expenses system in one call.

skills.name: OpenAI Fine‑Tuning
skills.description: Supervised fine‑tuning and preference fine‑tuning surfaces for tailoring models to a domain.
skills.example: A medical‑coding model is tuned on a curated corpus and outperforms zero‑shot on the firm's task.

skills.name: Gems
skills.description: Custom AI experts with a persona, instructions, and optional knowledge files — Gemini’s equivalent of Custom GPTs.
skills.example: A “Research Assistant” Gem is configured to always cite primary sources and respond in academic register.

skills.name: Deep Research
skills.description: Multi‑step autonomous research agent that browses the web, synthesises sources, and returns a cited long‑form report.
skills.trigger: “Deep Research” button · research X in depth
skills.example: A competitive landscape question returns a 10‑page report with 40 cited sources, generated in minutes.

skills.name: Live (Multimodal Live)
skills.description: Real‑time, low‑latency voice and video conversation — point your camera and talk to Gemini about what it sees.
skills.trigger: Microphone / camera icon in the mobile app
skills.example: A user holds their phone up to a broken circuit board and describes the fault aloud; Gemini diagnoses it live.

skills.name: Image Generation (Imagen 3)
skills.description: Native high‑fidelity image generation powered by Imagen 3, available directly in the chat surface.
skills.trigger: “Generate an image of…” · “Create a picture of…”
skills.example: A product‑mockup prompt returns a photorealistic render with accurate text and fine details.

skills.name: Veo 2 Video Generation
skills.description: AI‑powered video generation from text prompts, surfaced inside Gemini Advanced for eligible users.
skills.example: A nature‑documentary‑style prompt returns an 8‑second clip with cinematic camera movement.

skills.name: Canvas
skills.description: Side‑panel collaborative editing surface for long‑form documents and code — with inline revision and one‑click copy.
skills.trigger: Canvas icon in the toolbar · open in Canvas
skills.example: A 2,000‑word report draft is opened in Canvas; the user highlights a paragraph and asks Gemini to shorten it.

skills.name: Files in chat
skills.description: Upload and analyse documents (PDF, DOCX), images, audio, and video directly in the conversation.
skills.example: A 100‑page PDF contract is uploaded and summarised with key obligations extracted as a bullet list.

skills.name: NotebookLM
skills.description: Document‑grounded research assistant that answers only from your uploaded sources and can generate Audio Overviews (podcast‑style summaries).
skills.example: A researcher uploads 15 papers; NotebookLM answers questions with inline citations and generates a podcast episode summary.

skills.name: Web Search
skills.description: Real‑time Google Search grounding — Gemini queries the live web and cites its sources inline.
skills.example: “What did the Fed announce today?” returns a synthesised answer with linked news sources.

skills.name: Code Execution
skills.description: Python sandbox that runs code, produces charts, and manipulates uploaded files — powered by a Colab‑style kernel.
skills.trigger: Run this code · upload a CSV and ask to analyse
skills.example: An uploaded sales spreadsheet is cleaned with pandas, then a matplotlib chart is generated and returned.

skills.name: Data Analysis
skills.description: Conversational data analysis over uploaded spreadsheets and CSVs — summary statistics, charts, and trend commentary.
skills.trigger: Upload a file → Analyse this data
skills.example: A marketing CSV is profiled with descriptive stats, an outlier is flagged, and a revenue‑trend chart is produced.

skills.name: Workspace AI (Gemini for Workspace)
skills.description: Gemini embedded across Google Docs, Sheets, Slides, Gmail, and Meet — drafting, summarising, and formatting inside each app.
skills.trigger: Help me write · Summarise in Docs / Gmail · side panel in Sheets / Slides
skills.example: “Summarise this email thread and draft a follow‑up” runs inside Gmail, returning a reply draft in seconds.

skills.name: Google Extensions
skills.description: First‑party connectors to Google Search, Maps, YouTube, Hotels, Flights, and Workspace that Gemini queries during a conversation.
skills.trigger: Settings → Extensions → enable; or implicit from context
skills.example: “Find me a 4‑star hotel near the Colosseum for next Friday” uses the Hotels extension to return live results.

skills.name: Project Astra
skills.description: Real‑time multimodal agent that uses the phone camera as a continuous input stream — see, hear, and act on the live world.
skills.trigger: Astra mode in Gemini mobile app
skills.example: A user pans across a whiteboard diagram; Astra explains each component and suggests improvements verbally.

skills.name: Memory
skills.description: Persistent cross‑conversation facts Gemini learns and recalls to personalise future responses.
skills.example: Gemini recalls that a user prefers metric units and a formal tone across every new conversation.

skills.name: Projects
skills.description: Persistent workspaces that group related chats and shared context — Gemini remembers the background across all conversations inside a project.
skills.example: A “Thesis” project keeps all research chats, uploaded PDFs, and draft notes under one persistent context.

skills.name: Gemini API
skills.description: Google AI Studio API giving programmatic access to Gemini models — function calling, multimodal inputs, streaming, and long‑context up to 1M tokens.
skills.example: An app sends a 500‑page PDF and a question in one API call; the model answers with references to specific pages.

skills.name: Grounding with Google Search (API)
skills.description: API‑level Google Search grounding that injects live web results into model responses with inline citations.
skills.example: A product‑comparison request returns a structured answer sourced from current web pages, with cited URLs.

skills.name: Vertex AI Gemini
skills.description: Enterprise‑grade Gemini access via Google Cloud — private endpoints, VPC‑SC, audit logs, and SLA‑backed throughput.
skills.example: A regulated financial firm routes all model calls through Vertex for compliance logging and data residency.

skills.name: Gemini Files API
skills.description: Upload large files (up to 2 GB) once and reference them by URI across multiple API calls without re‑uploading.
skills.example: A 90‑minute audio interview is uploaded once; multiple summarise / transcribe calls reference the same file URI.

skills.name: Code Execution API
skills.description: API‑level sandboxed Python execution tool that lets Gemini models run generated code and return results programmatically.
skills.example: A model generates a data‑cleaning script, executes it against an uploaded CSV, and returns cleaned data in the same response.

skills.name: Gemini in Chrome
skills.description: Browser‑integrated Gemini assistant accessible from the Chrome address bar and side panel for page summarisation and Q&A.
skills.example: On a long news article, opening the side panel returns a three‑bullet summary and lets the user ask follow‑up questions.

skills.name: Extended Thinking
skills.description: Claude reasons through complex problems step‑by‑step using an explicit thinking budget before producing its final answer, surfacing its chain‑of‑thought as a collapsible block.
skills.trigger: Enabled automatically for difficult reasoning tasks on claude.ai; toggle via the 'Extended thinking' switch in model settings
skills.howto: In Claude.ai, open the model selector and enable the 'Extended thinking' toggle. For API access, set thinking: {type:'enabled', budget_tokens:8000} in your request. Claude will emit a thinking block before the response.
skills.example: Asked to find the flaw in a multi‑step math proof, Claude works through each step in its thinking block, catches the sign error in step 4, and returns a corrected proof.

skills.name: Deep Research
skills.description: Autonomous multi‑step web research agent that browses dozens of sources, synthesises findings, and delivers a cited long‑form research report.
skills.trigger: "Research …" button in ChatGPT · Deep Research mode selector
skills.howto: In ChatGPT, click the Deep Research button (or select it from the attachment menu). Describe your research question in detail. ChatGPT will show a live progress panel while it searches and reads sources, then produce a structured report with inline citations.
skills.example: Asked to compare the latest LLM benchmarks across frontier models, ChatGPT browses recent papers and leaderboards, then returns a 2 000‑word structured report with a comparison table and source list.

skills.name: Pro Search
skills.description: Enhanced search mode that triggers multi‑step clarification and deeper source analysis before answering, producing longer, more thorough responses with cited sources.
skills.trigger: Pro Search button · 'Explain in depth…' queries · automatic for complex questions
skills.howto: Click the 'Pro Search' toggle in the Perplexity query bar, or phrase your question to be detailed enough that Perplexity automatically activates it. The response will include a source panel with numbered citations.
skills.example: Asking 'What are the practical differences between RAG and fine‑tuning for production LLM apps?' triggers Pro Search, which reads multiple blog posts and papers before delivering a structured comparison.

skills.name: Spaces
skills.description: Collaborative workspaces that persist conversation threads, uploaded files, and custom AI personas — shareable with a team.
skills.trigger: Spaces tab → New Space · Invite members
skills.howto: Click 'Spaces' in the sidebar and create a new space. Upload reference documents, set a custom AI persona/system prompt, and invite collaborators. All searches and threads inside a space are scoped to the shared context.
skills.example: A research team creates a Space for a literature review project, uploads 30 PDFs, and each member asks questions that draw on the same document set.

skills.name: Perplexity Pages
skills.description: One‑click conversion of a Perplexity research thread into a shareable, richly formatted web page with sections, images, and citations.
skills.trigger: 'Share as Page' button after a search · Pages tab
skills.howto: After a Perplexity search produces a detailed answer, click 'Share as Page'. A formatted public page is created at perplexity.ai/page/… that you can share via link. You can edit the title and sections before publishing.
skills.example: After researching the history of transformer models, the user clicks 'Share as Page' and gets a polished article with an auto‑generated header image that they post to a company newsletter.

skills.name: Focus Mode
skills.description: Source‑scoped search that restricts results to a specific domain: Reddit, academic papers, YouTube, news, or your own files.
skills.trigger: Focus selector (All / Academic / YouTube / Reddit / Social / Writing / My Files)
skills.howto: Before submitting a query, click the Focus selector in the query bar and choose a domain. 'Academic' limits to arXiv, PubMed, and peer‑reviewed sources. 'Reddit' searches community discussions. 'My Files' limits to your uploaded documents.
skills.example: A user researching side effects of a drug selects 'Academic' focus, ensuring Perplexity only cites journal articles rather than web forums.

skills.name: Perplexity Assistant
skills.description: Mobile and desktop AI assistant that goes beyond search — booking, form‑filling, and multi‑app task execution using device permissions.
skills.trigger: Perplexity mobile app → Assistant tab · 'Do …' commands
skills.howto: In the Perplexity mobile app, open the Assistant tab. Grant the requested device permissions. Describe a real‑world task such as 'Book a table for 2 at an Italian restaurant near me tonight'. The assistant will browse, confirm details, and complete the action.
skills.example: User says 'Add the top 3 news stories today to my calendar as reminders'; the assistant reads news summaries and creates calendar events without the user touching the calendar app.

skills.name: Copilot in Microsoft 365
skills.description: AI assistant embedded natively in Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Outlook, and Teams — drafts, summarises, rewrites, and analyses content in context.
skills.trigger: Copilot button or M365 Chat pane in any Office app · Alt+I in Word
skills.howto: In any Microsoft 365 app with a Copilot licence, click the Copilot button in the ribbon. Describe what you need: 'Summarise this email thread', 'Create a first draft from these bullet points', or 'Explain the trend in column B'. Copilot has full document context.
skills.example: In Outlook, selecting a 40‑message thread and clicking 'Summarise thread' returns a three‑bullet digest with action items, letting the user reply in seconds.

skills.name: Copilot Designer
skills.description: AI image‑generation tool integrated into Microsoft 365 and Bing, powered by DALL·E 4, for creating graphics from text prompts.
skills.trigger: designer.microsoft.com · Copilot Image Creator in Bing · Insert → AI Image in PowerPoint
skills.howto: Go to designer.microsoft.com or use the Bing image creator. Describe the image you want. For PowerPoint, use Insert → AI Image and describe the graphic; it appears directly on the slide.
skills.example: A marketer types 'flat‑style illustration of a person at a laptop with glowing charts, teal colour scheme' and gets four polished options to use in a presentation.

skills.name: Copilot Pages
skills.description: Persistent, editable collaborative documents created from Copilot chat responses — a living canvas that teams can extend and share.
skills.trigger: 'Edit in Pages' button in Copilot chat · copilot.microsoft.com Pages tab
skills.howto: In Microsoft Copilot Chat (copilot.microsoft.com or in Teams), after getting a useful response click 'Edit in Pages'. A new Page is created that you and colleagues can continue editing. Changes sync in real time for all editors.
skills.example: After Copilot drafts a project brief from bullet points, the PM clicks 'Edit in Pages', shares the link with the team, and everyone refines it together in real time.

skills.name: Copilot Notebook
skills.description: Long‑context document workspace in Microsoft Copilot that accepts up to 18 000 words of input, ideal for detailed analysis and iterative drafting.
skills.howto: Open copilot.microsoft.com and click the Notebook tab. Paste in a long document, dataset, or set of notes (up to ~18 000 words) and then ask questions or request rewrites. The context persists for the session so you can iterate freely.
skills.example: A consultant pastes a 12 000‑word RFP into Notebook and asks Copilot to extract all scoring criteria, then to draft responses for each criterion.

skills.name: Claude Extended Thinking API
skills.description: API parameter that enables Claude's internal chain‑of‑thought — returns a thinking block alongside the final response for auditable, budget‑controlled deep reasoning.
skills.trigger: thinking: {type:'enabled', budget_tokens:N} in the Messages API request
skills.howto: Add thinking: {type:'enabled', budget_tokens:8000} to your /v1/messages API call. The response will include a content block of type 'thinking' before the final 'text' block. Increase budget_tokens (up to 32 000) for harder problems. Minimum budget is 1 000 tokens.
skills.example: A legal‑tech app sends a contract clause and asks for a risk analysis; the thinking block reveals the step‑by‑step legal reasoning while the text block returns a concise risk rating.

skills.name: Perplexity Sonar API
skills.description: OpenAI‑compatible REST API for Perplexity's real‑time web‑search‑augmented models — get cited, up‑to‑date answers in any application.
skills.howto: Obtain a Perplexity API key at perplexity.ai/settings/api. Use the OpenAI SDK or any HTTP client, pointing to https://api.perplexity.ai. Set model to 'sonar' (fast) or 'sonar-pro' (deep research). The response's citations array contains source URLs.
skills.example: A news aggregator app calls the Sonar API every hour with 'What are the top AI news stories today?' and parses the citations to build a sourced headline feed.

skills.name: DOCX Skill
skills.description: Official Anthropic skill pack that generates polished, editable Word documents from a prompt — headings, tables, bullets, and tracked changes.
skills.trigger: "Write me a report on…" · auto-discovered when a .docx output is needed
skills.example: Ask for a technical spec; the skill produces a formatted .docx with sections, a TOC, and consistent heading styles.

skills.name: PPTX Skill
skills.description: Official Anthropic skill pack that builds real, editable PowerPoint presentations with slide layouts, speaker notes, and branded styling.
skills.trigger: "Make me a deck about…" · auto-discovered for presentation requests
skills.example: A 10-slide investor deck with title, agenda, and chart slides is generated as a real .pptx file.

skills.name: XLSX Skill
skills.description: Official Anthropic skill pack that creates structured Excel workbooks with formulas, named ranges, charts, and multiple sheets.
skills.trigger: "Build me a budget spreadsheet" · auto-discovered for data/table requests
skills.example: A financial model with three sheets, linked formulas, and a chart is generated as a real .xlsx file.

skills.name: PDF Skill
skills.description: Official Anthropic skill pack that produces formatted PDF documents — reports, invoices, briefs — with consistent typography and layout.
skills.trigger: "Create a PDF report on…" · auto-discovered for PDF output requests
skills.example: A client-ready consulting brief is generated as a styled PDF with cover page and page numbers.

skills.name: Frontend Design Skill
skills.description: Official Anthropic skill pack for designing production-quality web UIs — component libraries, responsive layouts, and design systems.
skills.trigger: "Design a landing page for…" · "Build a dashboard component"
skills.example: A marketing landing page is designed with hero, features, and CTA sections using modern HTML/CSS.

skills.name: MCP Builder Skill
skills.description: Official Anthropic skill pack that scaffolds a working MCP server from a description — tools, resources, and transports included.
skills.example: Describe a GitHub MCP server and receive a complete, runnable server with tool definitions and transport config.

skills.name: Webapp Testing Skill
skills.description: Official Anthropic skill pack that writes and runs Playwright browser tests — end-to-end flows, accessibility checks, and screenshot assertions.
skills.trigger: "Test my login flow" · "Write e2e tests for…"
skills.example: Claude writes a full Playwright test suite for a checkout flow, runs it, and reports failures with annotated screenshots.

skills.name: Skill Creator Skill
skills.description: Official Anthropic meta-skill that authors new SKILL.md files — writes the frontmatter, instructions, and references from a plain description.
skills.example: Say "Create a skill that reviews Python for PEP 8" and receive a ready-to-use SKILL.md folder.

skills.name: Algorithmic Art Skill
skills.description: Official Anthropic creative skill that generates generative art scripts — SVG patterns, canvas animations, and mathematical visualisations.
skills.trigger: "Generate algorithmic art with…" · "Make a generative pattern"
skills.example: A Fibonacci spiral with colour gradients is produced as a self-contained HTML canvas animation.

skills.name: Brand Guidelines Skill
skills.description: Official Anthropic skill that enforces brand voice, tone, and visual rules across generated copy and designs.
skills.trigger: "Write in our brand voice" · "Check this copy against brand guidelines"
skills.example: Marketing copy is rewritten to match the brand's defined tone — concise, active, and jargon-free.

skills.name: Internal Comms Skill
skills.description: Official Anthropic skill for drafting internal communications — all-hands updates, incident reports, and policy announcements.
skills.trigger: "Write an all-hands email about…" · "Draft an incident post-mortem"
skills.example: A major outage post-mortem is structured with timeline, root cause, and action items in the correct company format.

skills.name: Canvas Design Skill
skills.description: Official Anthropic skill for producing visual designs — diagrams, mockups, and styled layouts — inside Claude's canvas surface.
skills.example: A mobile app mockup with navigation bar, card grid, and bottom tab bar is produced directly in canvas.

skills.name: Claude API Skill
skills.description: Official Anthropic skill that guides building with the Claude Messages API — prompt patterns, tool use, streaming, and batching best practices.
skills.trigger: "Build an app using the Claude API" · "How do I implement tool_use?"
skills.example: A customer-support bot is scaffolded with tool definitions, system prompt, and retry handling using the Claude SDK.

skills.name: Web Artifacts Builder Skill
skills.description: Official Anthropic skill for building elaborate multi-component web artifacts — interactive dashboards, data visualisations, and mini-apps.
skills.example: A multi-tab dashboard with live charts, a filterable data table, and a sidebar is produced as a self-contained HTML artifact.

skills.name: Slack GIF Creator Skill
skills.description: Official Anthropic skill that generates animated GIFs formatted and sized for Slack — from text prompts or data.
skills.example: An animated celebration GIF is generated for a team milestone and exported at Slack-optimal dimensions.

skills.name: Theme Factory Skill
skills.description: Official Anthropic skill that generates complete design tokens and theme files — colours, typography, spacing — for any brand or product.
skills.trigger: "Create a dark theme for…" · "Generate design tokens for…"
skills.example: A full dark-mode design system with CSS custom properties, Tailwind config, and component previews is generated from a brand brief.

skills.name: AI Research Skills
skills.description: Open-source library of 83+ structured research skills (Orchestra Research) — literature review, paper summarisation, experimental design, and citation management.
skills.example: Ask Claude to review recent papers on RAG and receive a structured literature matrix with key findings and citation links.

skills.name: Cybersecurity Skills
skills.description: Open-source library of 754 structured cybersecurity skills mapped to MITRE ATT&CK, NIST CSF 2.0, and D3FEND frameworks (mukul975).
skills.trigger: Copy skill folder to `.claude/skills/` · invoke by security domain
skills.example: Ask for a threat model for a web API and receive a structured report mapped to MITRE ATT&CK TTPs with mitigations.

skills.name: Awesome Claude Skills
skills.description: Community-curated registry of 100+ Claude Code SKILL.md packs — productivity, engineering, marketing, research, and domain-specific skills.
skills.trigger: Browse and clone individual skill folders from the repo
skills.example: Pick the 'aws-skills' pack, drop it in `.claude/skills/`, and Claude Code gains AWS CDK and cost-optimisation expertise.

skills.name: Plan Mode
skills.description: A read-only mode that disables all file writes so OpenCode can propose and iterate on an implementation plan before touching any code.
skills.trigger: Press Tab to toggle Plan/Build mode
skills.example: User presses Tab -> types 'Refactor auth to use JWT' -> OpenCode returns a 5-step plan with no file changes -> user says 'also handle refresh tokens' -> plan updates -> Tab back to Build -> 'implement it'

skills.name: Undo / Redo
skills.description: Snapshot-based undo and redo that rolls back or reapplies all file changes the agent made during the last operation.
skills.example: Agent refactors the wrong module -> user types /undo -> all files restored to original state -> user adds 'only touch auth.ts' -> resubmits

skills.name: Agent Skills (SKILL.md)
skills.description: Reusable instruction files the agent discovers and loads on demand. Place a SKILL.md file in .opencode/skills/<name>/ to define a repeatable workflow.
skills.trigger: Create .opencode/skills/<name>/SKILL.md with name and description frontmatter
skills.example: Skill 'git-release' defines steps for tagging and writing changelogs -> user asks 'cut a release for v2.1.0' -> agent loads the skill and follows the steps exactly

skills.name: Session Share
skills.description: Generates a public shareable link to the full agent conversation including all prompts, reasoning steps, and file diffs.
skills.example: Agent implements a new API endpoint -> developer types /share -> link copied -> pasted in PR description so reviewers can see every decision the agent made

skills.name: MCP Server Integration
skills.description: Connect external tools via MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers by configuring them in opencode.json.
skills.trigger: Add an mcp key to opencode.json with server definitions
skills.example: Configure a Postgres MCP server -> ask 'show me users created this week' -> agent queries the DB directly through the MCP tool

skills.name: Custom Commands
skills.description: Define custom slash commands for repetitive workflows as markdown files in .opencode/commands/ or in opencode.json.
skills.example: Command 'fix-types' defined as 'Find all TypeScript type errors and fix them' -> user types /fix-types -> agent runs the full workflow immediately

skills.name: GitHub Actions Integration
skills.description: Trigger OpenCode in CI/CD by commenting /opencode or /oc on any GitHub issue or PR. Supports automatic PR review, issue triage, and scheduled runs.
skills.trigger: Comment /opencode <task> or /oc <task> on a GitHub issue or PR
skills.example: Reviewer comments '/opencode add input validation to the form fields' -> agent runs in GitHub Actions -> commits the changes -> replies with a summary

skills.name: Custom Agents
skills.description: Define specialized agents with their own model, system prompt, and tool permissions using markdown files in .opencode/agents/.
skills.trigger: Create .opencode/agents/<name>.md with YAML frontmatter defining the agent
skills.example: Agent 'security-reviewer' defined with read-only permissions and a system prompt focused on OWASP checks -> user audits a new endpoint before merging

skills.name: LSP Integration
skills.description: Connect Language Server Protocol servers so the agent can use go-to-definition, find references, and get real-time diagnostics.
skills.trigger: Add an lsp key to opencode.json with server definitions
skills.example: Agent needs to rename a function -> calls the LSP find-references tool and gets exact locations including dynamic call sites

skills.name: Plugins
skills.description: Extend OpenCode with custom executable tools the LLM can call. Place JS/TS files in .opencode/plugins/ or load from npm via the plugin key in opencode.json.
skills.trigger: Place a plugin file in .opencode/plugins/ or add plugin key in opencode.json
skills.example: Plugin defines a 'deploy-preview' tool that calls Vercel's API -> agent runs tests, then calls deploy-preview -> gets the URL and pastes it into the PR description

skills.name: Plan Mode
skills.description: A read-only planning mode where Codex proposes an implementation plan without writing any files. Iterate on approach before committing to execution.
skills.trigger: $plan or toggle Plan Mode in the thread composer
skills.example: User types $plan -> 'Migrate this Express API to Fastify' -> Codex returns a 7-step migration plan -> user asks 'keep the existing middleware' -> plan updates -> user says 'go ahead'

skills.name: Goal Mode
skills.description: Long-running autonomous task execution toward a measurable finish line. Codex keeps working, across hours or days, until the goal's success criterion is met.
skills.example: User sets goal: 'Migrate the Python CLI to Rust, done when all existing tests pass' -> Codex works for hours -> pauses when it needs a decision -> user resumes after lunch

skills.name: Agent Skills (SKILL.md)
skills.description: Reusable workflow packages with instructions, scripts, and resources. Codex auto-discovers skills from .agents/skills/ and loads the right one when your task matches.
skills.trigger: Type $skill-name or $ to browse skills; or describe a task that matches a skill's description
skills.example: Skill 'linear-pr' packages steps for creating a Linear ticket and linking a PR -> user asks 'create a ticket for this bug and open a PR' -> Codex loads the skill and follows the exact steps

skills.name: Skill Creator
skills.description: A built-in system skill that interviews you about a workflow and generates a ready-to-use SKILL.md file for it.
skills.example: User types $skill-creator -> answers 3 questions about their deploy workflow -> Codex writes a complete SKILL.md with instructions and saves it to .agents/skills/deploy/

skills.name: Skill Installer
skills.description: A built-in system skill that installs curated third-party skills from the community skills registry into your local Codex setup.
skills.example: User types '$skill-installer linear' -> Codex installs the Linear skill -> user can now say '$linear create a ticket for this issue' in any thread

skills.name: Thread Automations
skills.description: Scheduled recurring wake-up calls that return to the same Codex thread on a cron-like schedule, keeping a workflow running while you're away.
skills.trigger: Set up via Settings -> Automations; attach to a pinned thread
skills.example: Chief-of-Staff thread set to run every 30 minutes: checks Slack and Gmail for unanswered messages, researches answers, drafts replies

skills.name: Durable Threads (Pinned)
skills.description: Long-running Codex threads that preserve working context, decisions, and preferences across repeated sessions. Accessible instantly via Cmd+1 through Cmd+9.
skills.trigger: Pin a thread via the thread menu; use Cmd+1-9 to jump back
skills.example: User pins a 'Release Thread' -> each sprint, opens it with Cmd+1 -> Codex already knows the team's release process, past blockers, and preferences

skills.name: Worktree Mode
skills.description: Isolate Codex changes in a Git worktree so the agent can work on a parallel branch without touching your current working files.
skills.trigger: Choose 'Worktree' when starting a new thread in the Codex app
skills.example: User starts a Worktree thread -> asks Codex to try a risky refactor -> Codex works in isolation -> user reviews the diff -> decides to merge or discard

skills.name: MCP Server Integration
skills.description: Connect external tools and services via Model Context Protocol servers. Shared across Codex app, CLI, and IDE Extension automatically.
skills.trigger: Open Settings -> MCP in the Codex app or configure in CLI config
skills.example: Add the OpenAI Docs MCP server -> ask 'how do I use streaming with the Responses API?' -> Codex fetches live docs through the MCP tool

skills.name: Voice Input
skills.description: Dictate prompts directly into Codex using your microphone. Useful for vague or rough thoughts that are natural to say but awkward to type.
skills.trigger: Hold Ctrl+M while the composer is visible
skills.example: User holds Ctrl+M -> says 'I think there's a race condition somewhere in the auth flow, go look' -> Codex transcribes and starts investigating

skills.name: Steering & Queuing
skills.description: Steering interrupts an in-flight task with new direction before it finishes. Queuing adds the next task to run after the current step completes.
skills.trigger: Type or speak mid-task to steer; add a follow-up message to queue
skills.example: Codex is reviewing a page -> user steers: 'make that header smaller' -> Codex adjusts mid-task. User queues: 'once done, send the preview link to the reviewer in Slack'

skills.name: Shared Memory (AGENTS.md + Vault)
skills.description: Durable context stored outside any single thread so future sessions can resume from something explicit. Define it via AGENTS.md and a structured vault folder.
skills.trigger: Create AGENTS.md at the repo root or vault root with memory routing instructions
skills.example: AGENTS.md tells Codex to update vault/people/ and vault/projects/ after every session -> next session picks up the last decision, owner, and blocker
